title: Lachemilla hispidula
intro: Lachemilla hispidula, also known as the woolly rock cinquefoil, is a low-growing, spreading perennial plant native to the Andes mountains in South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help to soothe wounds, reduce fever, and protect against infections. The bioactive constituents of Lachemilla hispidula include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often used to make infusions, tinctures, and salves, which are used to treat various conditions, including skin irritations, coughs, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Lachemilla hispidula is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rosaceae. It belongs to the genus Lachemilla, which consists of several species of small, delicate plants. The plant is native to the Andes mountains in South America, where it grows in moist, rocky areas. Lachemilla hispidula is a member of the order Rosales and the class Magnoliopsida. Its classification is further detailed in the kingdom Plantae, within the domain Eukaryota.
